title: ML Project Structure
description: Andrew Ng-inspired ML project strategy — single-number metrics, human-level performance, error analysis, train/dev/test distributions, bias vs variance, end-to-end vs pipeline learning, orthogonalization, and ablation studies.
details: Structuring ML projects systematically separates the signal from the noise. Start with a single-number evaluation metric and establish human-level performance (HLP) as a Bayes error proxy — HLP tells you if your model suffers from avoidable bias or variance. Error analysis on 100 dev examples reveals the highest-impact areas for improvement. The train-dev set concept diagnoses data mismatch. Orthogonalization ensures each hyperparameter controls exactly one axis of performance. These principles, from Andrew Ng's 'Structuring ML Projects' course, form the bedrock of systematic ML engineering.

sections.title: Setting Up ML Strategy
sections.description:
[Intuition] If you have to balance multiple metrics, make one the optimizing metric and turn the rest into satisficing constraints. For example, optimize F1 score (you want this as high as possible) subject to latency < 100ms and model size < 500MB.

[Practical Workflow] 1. Pick a single-number metric (F1, accuracy, NDCG). 2. Define satisficing constraints (latency, memory, fairness). 3. Split data 60/20/20 train/dev/test. 4. Set dev and test from the same distribution — this is critical for reliable evaluation.

sections.title: Human-Level Performance (HLP)
sections.description:
[Concrete Numbers] For medical imaging, HLP is 3% — expert radiologists achieve 97% accuracy. If your model gets 8% error, then avoidable bias = 8% - 3% = 5% (focus on bias reduction). Once the model reaches 3%, further gains come from reducing variance (more data, regularization).

[Practical Workflow] 1. Get human labels on your task (multiple experts for consensus). 2. Compute HLP. 3. If training error > HLP → focus on bias. 4. If dev error > training error → focus on variance.
sections.codeBlock:
# Diagnosis: Bias vs Variance
# HLP = human-level performance (Bayes error proxy)
# training_error = error on training set
# dev_error = error on dev set

def diagnose_bias_variance(hlp, training_error, dev_error):
    avoidable_bias = training_error - hlp
    variance = dev_error - training_error

    print(f"Human-level performance: {hlp:.1%}")
    print(f"Avoidable bias: {avoidable_bias:.1%}")
    print(f"Variance: {variance:.1%}")

    if avoidable_bias > variance and avoidable_bias > 0.05:
        print("  Focus on REDUCING BIAS:")
        print("   - Larger model (more layers/units)")
        print("   - Better optimization (Adam, LR tuning)")
        print("   - Better initialization")
        print("   - Longer training")
    elif variance > 0.05:
        print("  Focus on REDUCING VARIANCE:")
        print("   - More training data")
        print("   - Regularization (L2, dropout)")
        print("   - Data augmentation")
        print("   - Early stopping")
    else:
        print("[Key Takeaway] Model is well-balanced")

# Example: medical imaging
# HLP = 3%, training = 8%, dev = 15%
diagnose_bias_variance(0.03, 0.08, 0.15)
# Output: avoidable_bias=5%, variance=7%
# Balanced recommendation: try both, but variance is slightly larger issue

sections.title: Error Analysis
sections.description:
[Intuition] Don't spend weeks reducing one type of error if it's only 5% of mistakes. The 80/20 rule applies — find the biggest bucket and fix that first. Error analysis is the ML equivalent of profiling before optimizing.

[Practical Workflow] 1. Sample 100 misclassified dev examples. 2. Categorize each (blurry, occlusion, wrong label, rare object, etc.). 3. Count per category. 4. Fix the biggest bucket. 5. Repeat.
sections.codeBlock:
# Error analysis: categorize 100 misclassified dev examples
import pandas as pd

errors = [
    {'image': 'img_001.jpg', 'category': 'blurry', 'true': 'dog', 'pred': 'cat'},
    {'image': 'img_002.jpg', 'category': 'occlusion', 'true': 'car', 'pred': 'truck'},
    # ... 98 more
]

df = pd.DataFrame(errors)
category_counts = df['category'].value_counts()
print(f"Error breakdown (total: {len(df)}):")
print(category_counts)

# Priority: fix the biggest bucket first
# Blurry: 35 errors. If we reduce blurry errors by 50%, accuracy improves by 17.5%
# Wrong label: 15 errors. If we fix all, only 15% improvement.
#  Invest in deblurring augmentation first!

sections.title: Train/Dev/Test Distributions
sections.description:
[Intuition] The train-dev set is the key diagnostic tool. It's a subset of training data, drawn from the same distribution as training, but used as a dev set. If your model performs well on train-dev but poorly on the actual dev set, you have data mismatch — the training distribution differs from the target distribution.

[Interview Angle] Common mistake: training data is web-scraped (clean, high-res), but production data is from mobile phones (noisy, low-res). The model performs great on dev (which shares training distribution) but fails in production. The train-dev set catches this.

sections.title: Bias vs Variance in Deep Learning
sections.description:
[Intuition] The classic bias-variance tradeoff doesn't fully apply to deep learning. Large models can have low bias AND low variance simultaneously — they're flexible enough to fit any function (low bias) and generalize well with enough data and regularization (low variance).

[Practical Workflow] Underfit → bigger model, better optimizer, better initialization. Overfit → more data, regularization (L2, dropout, label smoothing), data augmentation.

sections.title: End-to-End vs Pipeline Learning
sections.description:
[Intuition] E2E needs lots of (X,Y) data pairs. Pipeline can use specialized data for each component. Use pipeline when data is limited, when you have strong domain priors, or when each component needs individual debugging.

[Concrete Numbers] Speech recognition: E2E (audio → text) works great when you have millions of hours of transcribed audio. But for medical diagnosis where labeled data is scarce, a pipeline (segmentation → feature extraction → classification) with hand-designed features outperforms E2E.

sections.title: Orthogonalization
sections.description:
[Intuition] Each tunable knob controls exactly ONE thing. If changing the learning rate simultaneously affects training error AND generalization, you have non-orthogonal knobs. Good knobs only affect one axis of performance.

[Interview Angle] "Your model has 85% accuracy. How do you decide what to improve?" — First, establish HLP and compute avoidable bias. Then run error analysis on 100 dev examples to find the biggest error category. Check if it's a data problem (mismatch) or a model problem (bias/variance). The systematic approach: measure first, then act. Don't randomly change things — orthogonalize.

sections.title: Ablation Studies
sections.description:
[Intuition] Ablation studies tell you what actually matters. Remove one component at a time and measure the performance drop. This prevents you from adding complexity without justification.

[Concrete Numbers] Ablation on a Transformer: remove positional encoding → -15% accuracy. Reduce heads from 8 to 4 → -5%. Remove feedforward layer → -30%. The FFN matters most — invest optimization effort there.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

# Sensitivity analysis: ablate one component, measure performance
components = {
    'full_model': 0.92,
    'wo_positional_encoding': 0.78,   # -15%
    'wo_ffn': 0.64,                    # -30%
    'wo_attention': 0.45,              # -51%
    'half_heads': 0.87,                # -5%
    'no_dropout': 0.90,                # -2% (legacy: full has dropout)
}

baseline = components['full_model']
print(f"Baseline: {baseline:.1%}")
for name, score in sorted(components.items()):
    if name == 'full_model':
        continue
    drop = baseline - score
    print(f"{-name:25s}: {score:.1%}  (drop: {drop:.1%})")

# Insight: FFN and attention are critical. Heads can be halved with only 5% loss.
# Action: if you need to reduce model size, halving heads is safer than reducing FFN.

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — ML Project Structure
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] These questions test your ability to systematically debug and improve ML systems. The key is having a structured process, not just intuition.

  1. Apple L4: "Your model has 85% accuracy. How do you decide what to improve?" — Step 1: Establish HLP (get human labels). Step 2: Compute avoidable bias (training error - HLP) and variance (dev error - training error). Step 3: Error analysis on 100 dev misclassifications to find biggest error bucket. Step 4: If bias issue, improve model capacity. If variance issue, add data/regularization. If data mismatch, collect target-like data. Never guess — measure first.
  2. Google L5: "Explain the concept of avoidable bias and how you'd compute it." — Avoidable bias = training error - human-level performance (HLP). HLP is a proxy for Bayes error (the lowest possible error). If training error > HLP, your model is not fitting the training set well enough. Example: HLP=5%, training error=15% → avoidable bias=10%. Focus on model capacity, optimization, architecture.
  3. Amazon L5: "When would you use end-to-end deep learning instead of a pipeline?" — E2E is better when: (a) you have massive datasets (100K+ examples), (b) the task doesn't decompose cleanly into subtasks, (c) you want to avoid compounding errors from intermediate modules. Pipeline is better when: (a) data is scarce per component but you have domain expertise, (b) each subtask needs its own evaluation, (c) you need interpretability or guarantees per stage.
  4. Meta E5: "How do you detect data mismatch between training and production?" — Create a train-dev set (subset of training data, same distribution, used as dev). If model performs well on train-dev but poorly on the actual dev set (which matches production), you have data mismatch. Fix by: collecting production-like data, data augmentation to simulate production conditions, or domain adaptation techniques.
  5. Google L4: "What is orthogonalization and why does it matter for ML?" — Orthogonalization means each hyperparameter affects exactly one performance axis. Bad example: changing model size affects both training error AND inference latency — you can't independently control these. Good practice: separate knobs for training (size, LR, optimizer) and deployment (quantization, pruning). Ensures you can debug and improve each axis independently.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• Andrew Ng — Coursera "Structuring Machine Learning Projects" (Course 3 of Deep Learning Specialization)
  • Andrew Ng — "Machine Learning Yearning" (book)
  • CS229 Lecture Notes — Bias-Variance Tradeoff, Regularization, Model Selection
  • Kohavi et al. 1995 — A Study of Cross-Validation and Bootstrap for Accuracy Estimation and Model Selection
  • Friedman et al. 2001 — The Elements of Statistical Learning (Ch. 7: Model Assessment and Selection)
